cards.front: What is the approximate ON voltage of a silicon diode in logic circuit analysis?
cards.back: VON ≈ 0.8V (from Vγ ≈ 0.6V plus the forward resistance drop IDRf ≈ 0.2V)

cards.front: How is a diode modelled when it is OFF?
cards.back: As an open circuit — no current flows. The reverse leakage current is negligible for logic circuit analysis.

cards.front: In the 2-input diode AND gate, how are the diodes oriented?
cards.back: Anodes connected to the common output node, cathodes facing the inputs (A and B). A pull-up resistor connects the output to +Vcc.

cards.front: When is the output of the diode AND gate HIGH?
cards.back: Only when BOTH inputs are HIGH (+Vcc). Both diodes are reverse-biased (OFF), and the pull-up resistor pulls the output to +Vcc.

cards.front: What is the output voltage when any input of the diode AND gate is LOW?
cards.back: Approximately 0.8V (LOW). The diode connected to the low input conducts, clamping the output to VON ≈ 0.8V.
LOW voltage (≈ 0V) = logic 0 (boolean zero). HIGH voltage (+Vcc) = logic 1 (boolean one). Higher voltage = higher logic value.

cards.front: What logic gate results from reversing the diode orientations (anodes at inputs)?
cards.back: An OR gate. A HIGH input forward-biases its diode and pulls the output HIGH. Output is LOW only when all inputs are LOW.

cards.front: What is the main limitation of diode logic gates?
cards.back: No signal restoration — each stage degrades the voltage levels by approximately 0.8V. After cascading several stages, logic levels become indistinguishable.

cards.front: What is the complete ON model of a silicon diode?
cards.back: An ideal diode in series with a voltage source Vγ (≈ 0.6V) and a forward resistance Rf. Total: VON = Vγ + ID × Rf.
To overcome diode logic's limitations — DTL adds a BJT that restores signal levels (amplifies), giving proper HIGH and LOW outputs regardless of the number of cascaded stages.
